content.assessment: Shifting finance architecture away from diagnosis-gated multipliers touches governance and accountability more than fundamental legal authority, since funding formulas are typically adjustable via administrative/financial regulation rather than requiring wholesale amendment of SNI status law. Stronger conditionality tying grants to documented service delivery would improve accountability and enforceability, directly addressing weak current conditionality, but the underlying incentive theory remains untested against Hungarian evidence.
opportunities: Stricter delivery-based conditionality could be paired with monitoring infrastructure to enforce actual receipt of legally prescribed habilitation/therapy hours
strengths: Improved conditionality directly targets a documented accountability gap: SNI-linked grants and the 2-3x pupil-count multiplier currently lack strong enforcement tying funds to actual service delivery
Funding-formula change is more administratively tractable than restructuring the categorical/legal-status architecture itself
threats: Removing diagnosis-linked funding without corresponding legal change to entitlement structures could create a mismatch between formal SNI/BTMN legal status and actual resource allocation, generating new accountability ambiguities
weaknesses: No causal evidence links Hungary's specific financing mechanism to the identification rise, so the legal-governance rationale rests on plausible but unproven incentive theory
Poland's contrasting trajectory (expansion rather than removal of disability-weighted funding) shows no convergent governance model exists to validate this approach
